canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, lexical diversity is most commonly defined as the variety of words and phrases, specifically n-grams, used within a collection of texts. Some papers offer slightly different framings, describing it as a model's capacity to generate varied vocabulary or focusing on the richness of "lexical types." The construct is operationalized through specific metrics; one paper notes that the "fraction of unique n-grams is a straightforward measure," while another uses vocd-D for its assessment. The provided research presents conflicting findings regarding the lexical diversity of LLM-generated text compared to human writing. For instance, one study reports that "Writing with InstructGPT reduces lexical diversity." In contrast, other work finds that "the collective LLM author has a greater lexical diversity than the human authors" and that synthetic data can be higher in diversity than its human-generated counterparts. Beyond this comparison, generating lexically diverse text is presented as a desirable model capability, with some work associating it with more expressive sentences and an "enhanced capability to fool LLM-generated text detectors."
papers: Circuit Stability Characterizes Language Model Generalization
Improving Contextual Faithfulness of Large Language Models via Retrieval Heads-Induced Optimization
Blind Men and the Elephant: Diverse Perspectives on Gender Stereotypes in Benchmark Datasets
MUZO: Leveraging Multiple Queries and Momentum for Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models
ToM-SSI: Evaluating Theory of Mind in Situated Social Interactions
Achieving Human Parity in Content-Grounded Datasets Generation
Does Writing with Language Models Reduce Content Diversity?

definitions.text: The variety of words and phrases (n-grams) used within a collection of texts.
